rulings.Case Title: HOEM & ASSOCIATES INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. UNIVERSITY OF THE PACIFIC ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2019, Line 5, Motion To Reopen Discovery Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The start time for the hearing is 10 am. Attorneys for parties affected by resolution of the issue of inspection of the previously obtained flooring core samples must appear in person. Other counsel may appear by phone. To be addressed by counsel is prejudice if the subject flooring core samples are made available for inspection. If inspection is granted the terms and conditions of the inspection will be established. The Court intends to set a date by which expert witness Blatchley's deposition must be completed. Prior to the hearing defendant Hoem is directed to inquire of its expert if there are date restrictions for his deposition appearance. The Court will also hear argument and rule whether Hoem's further Request for Production of Documents permitted by Discovery Order dated October 3, 2019 meets the scope of inquiry and limitations therein. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GREG HERMAN VS. PACIFIC HEIGHTS TOWERS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Compliance With Court Order And For Sanctions, Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof, And Declaration Of Mark B. Chernev
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2019, Line 1, CROSS COMPLAINANT PACIFIC HEIGHTS TOWERS, LLC'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Court Order And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion to compel compliance with the September 6, 2019 Discovery Order is denied. The monetary sanction awarded was paid subsequent to filing of the motion. Both parties' request for sanctions is denied. Payment of a monetary sanction arising from a discovery dispute is enforced through collection of judgment procedures. C.C.P. 2033.290 is not applicable. Defendant cites no authority in its Notice of Motion or Points and Authorities for the proposition further sanctions can be awarded to enforce payment. See C.C.P. 2023.040. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ICON INC, A NEVADA CORPORATION VS. TADICH GRILL, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Leave To Amend; And Motion
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANTS TADICH GRILL, INC., MICHAEL BUICH's Motion For Leave To Amend. Defendants and Cross-Complainants Tadich Grill, Inc. and Michael Buich's motion for leave to amend the Second Amended Cross-Complaint is granted. Granting the motion comports with California's liberal policy of allowing amendments to pleadings. (See, e.g., Howard v. County of San Diego (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1422, 1428 ["The policy favoring amendment is so strong that it is a rare case in which denial of leave to amend can be justified."].) If defendants wish to challenge the validity of the amended claims on limitations, waiver, or other grounds, they may do so by way of demurrer or motion for summary adjudicatoin. (See Brown v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 824, 843 ["The 'better course of action' is to allow a plaintiff to amend the [cross-]complaint 'and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings.'"].) Plaintiff and Cross-Defendants have not shown they would be prejudiced by allowing the amendment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD-STARTX FUND, LLC ET AL VS. MEDWHAT.COM INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Impose Discovery Sanctions For Failure To Comply With Discovery Order (Cal. Civ. Proc. 2023.010 And 2023.030)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF STANFORD-STARTX FUND, LLC'S Motion To Impose Discovery Sanctions For Failure To Comply With Discovery Order (Cal. Civ. Proc. 2023.010 And 2023.030) Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Parties to appear and address the status of defendant's compliance with the June 25, 2019 Discovery Order. The defendant served supplemental responses on October 22, 2019. The court will hear oral argument focused on the extent the late-served responses meet the terms of the Order. The opposition to the motion filed by defendant is not a cogent response. The defendant will be given an opportunity at oral argument to supplement his position with a focus on the discovery issues. Going forward the defendant is advised he will be held to the same litigation standards as an attorney. See: Kobayashi v. Superior Court, 175 Cal.App.4th 536 (2009) and Rappleyea v. Campbell, 8 Cal.4th 975, 985 (1994) ["A doctrine generally requiring or permitting exceptional treatment of parties who represent themselves would lead to a quagmire in the trial courts and would be unfair to the other parties to litigation."] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JONATHAN KORZEKWA VS. ENCORE KARAOKE LOUNGE, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Of Oceanic Latu, For A Protective Order Prohibiting Deponent From Refusing To Answer Questions, And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF JONATHAN KORZEKWA'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Oceanic Latu, For A Protective Order Prohibiting Deponent From Refusing To Answer Questions, And For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Parties to appear. The Court will rule on the deposition question which resulted in suspension of deposition questioning. See Ev. Code 788. The record does not support issuance of an order requiring defendant's counsel to prospectively "refrain from instructing the deponent not to answer questions, unless to protect a privilege or seek a protective order". Defense counsel's written commitment for the continued deposition of Oceanic Latu that he will "follow California law and CCP". See: CCP §2025.460 and 87 CA 4th 1006. Stewart v. Colonial Western Agency, Inc. (2001) is sufficient. The Order to be entered herein shall reference to the commitment which shall apply to all future depositions noticed by either party. A firm date for completion of the deposition of Oceanic Latu will be set. Both parties' requests for sanctions is denied. The suspension of questioning was properly and well-handled by both counsel. The witness is not a party in the action. The record reflects multiple attempts by defense counsel to promptly set a date for resumption of questioning. Counsel will not be sanctioned for a witnesses' lack of cooperation in deposition scheduling. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GHILOTTI BROS., INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT, INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT, INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Synergy Project Management, Inc.'s demurrer is overruled in its entirety. The demurrer to the breach of contract cause of action is overruled. Plaintiff is not required to attach a copy of the contract or plead its terms verbatim to state a claim. (Construction Protective Services, Inc. v. TIG Specialty Ins. Co. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 189, 199 ["a plaintiff may plead the legal effect of the contract rather than its precise language."]; Scolinos v. Kolts (1995) 37 Cal.App.4th 635, 640 ["A contract should be pleaded either in haec verba or according to its legal intendment and effect."].) To plead a contract according to its legal effect, the complaining party should allege the making and then proceed to allege the substance of its relevant terms. (See Pneucrete Corp. v. United States Fidelity & Guaranty Co., (1935) 7 Cal.App.2d 733, 741-742; McKell v. Washington Mut. Inc. (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1489.) Here, plaintiff alleges the substance of the relevant terms and states a claim. (Complaint, 9-13.) Plaintiff's failure to attach the contract to the complaint even though it is referenced as an exhibit is a minor error that does not render the complaint uncertain. The demurrer to the breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing claim is overruled. While there appears to be some overlap between this claim and the breach of contract claim, the allegations do not show that the implied covenant claim merely restates the breach of contract claim. Because the claim is not clearly superfluous, the demurrer is overruled. The demurrer to the breach of the contractual indemnity provision is overruled. "Although indemnity generally relates to third party claims, 'this general rule does not apply if the parties to a contract use the term 'indemnity' to include direct liability as well as third party liability.'" (Zalkind v. Ceradyne, Inc. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th1010, 1024.) Here, the indemnity clause provides that "[t]o the greatest extent permitted by law, [Defendant] shall…indemnify and hold harmless [Plaintiff]…from and against all…damages, costs, losses, and expenses." (Complaint, 37.) This language is broad enough to include a direct indemnity claim. (See Hot Rods, LLC v. Northrop Grumman Sys.Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 1166, 1181-1182 ["if the language was meant only to cover third party claims, there seems little point in including the phrase 'damages, costs, losses' when those would all have been covered already…The extremely broad language here, such as 'any claims, ... damages, costs, losses' does not suggest the parties intended to limit the provision to third party claims."] The contract is reasonably susceptible to Plaintiff's interpretation and the court accepts that interpretation on demurrer. (See Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239 ["While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
